title: MALDI-TOF MS library development for insects
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"chign","text":"Matrix-assisted laser desorption-ionisation time of flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF MS) is a powerful analytical method that has been used extensively to identify sample ions of complex mixtures, and biological samples such as proteins, tissues and microorganisms. MALDI-TOF MS has revolutionised clinical microbiology with accurate, rapid, and inexpensive species-level identification of microbes. MALDI-TOF MS technology generates spectral signatures and matches them to a library of similar organisms using bioinformatics pattern matching. The use of MALDI-TOF MS for entomological samples has been explored by multiple groups with proven efficacy at differentiating between closely related species, as well as detecting pathogens in different vectors.  The low cost per sample processing, rapid turnaround and robustness are attractive for surveillance of vector control programs. Libraries are built in-house for institutional usage, although a multi-user platform with sharing of spectra and data would be attractive, it is not yet available. Here, we outline a stepwise approach for creating an in-house MALDI-TOF MS library and subsequent query,\nusing malaria vector species identification as a case study for entomological samples.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Morphological identification
Separate Culicine and Anopheles mosquitoes based on identification keys [1–3].
Identify female Anopheles mosquitoes at the species level using dichotomous morphological keys [2–4]
Sort each sample based on their physiological status (blood fed, unfed, gravid, and half gravid)
Assign a unique identifier to each sample and store under appropriate conditions
Sample preparation
Place an individual sample on clean white roll paper

Using a clean and sterile scalpel, dissect the mosquito into the following body compartments: legs and wings (L/W), Cephalothorax (Head and Thorax (HT)), and abdomen (ABD) (for blood-fed mosquitoes) (Figure 1) . Discard abdomens of unfed mosquitoes
Subdivide HT into two longitudinal halves.
Store the different compartments in separate microcentrifuge tubes labelled with the same identification code
One half of the HT is screened for Plasmodium infection status and species identification using PCR. Legs and wings are stored as backup samples if HT is used for Plasmodium detection using ELISA
DNA extraction
Extract DNA from the HT or L/W using the Chelex protocol as previously described [6,7]
Species identification (cocktail PCR)
Amplify the genomic DNA using An. gambiae s.l [8] and An. funestus s.l [9] species-specific diagnostic primers described by Scott et al (1993) and Koekemoer et al (2002) respectively.

Subject all the unamplified samples and those to be used for MALDI-TOF MS database creation to sequencing targeting ITS2 and COI regions as previously described by Karisa et al (2024) and Lobo et al (2015) [10,11]
Plasmodium detection
Use the CSP ELISA procedure outlined in the MR4 guidelines (2014)
Alternatively, use Plasmodium PCR assay such as those described by Echeverry et al (2017) or Snounou (1993 & 1995) [12–14]
Protein extraction and plate loading
Add 15 µl of 70% (v/v) formic acid and 15 µl of 50% (v/v) acetonitrile (ACN) to the vial containing half of the head and thorax
Add a pinch of 106 µm acid wash glass beads until the glass beads are half full of the solvent in the vial
Homogenise sample in a Tissue Lyser II at 30Hz for 1 min for three cycles [10]
Centrifuge the samples at 13,000 rpm for 1 min to separate the debris from the proteins
Load one microliter (1 µl) of the sample on the MALDI-target plate in quadruplicate
Include one spot of Bacterial Test Standard (BTS) preparation as the positive control and four spots matrix only as the negative control
Allow the plate to air-dry at room temperature for 10-15 min

Overlay each sample spot on the plate with one µl of saturated matrix solution (composed of 0.25g α-cyano-4-hydroxycynnamic acid, acetonitrile (50% (v/v), trifluoroacetic acid (2.5% (v/v)) and 47.5% LC-MS grade water)
Allow the plate to dry again at room temperaturefor 10-15 min
Spectra acquisition
Load the plate into the Microflex instrument
Acquire spectra using the FlexControl software (Bruker Daltonics) with default settings or adjust the laser power (nitrogen laser) accordingly

Obtain spectra of mass ranges 2-20 kDa in a positive linear mode at a frequency of 60 Hz, an acceleration voltage of 20 kV, 200 ns extraction delay time, and a maximum laser power energy of 50%. Optimise the laser power until high quality spectra is achieved
Each spectrum is generated from 40 laser shots in six regions of the sample spot
Spectra analysis

Using FlexAnalysis software (Bruker Daltonics) [15], visually assess the spectra quality by checking general peak intensity (high intensity), the smoothness of the peaks, the flatness of the baseline and its reproducibility compared to other spectra of the same categories
Remove all the poor-quality spectra and flatlines from further processing
Load all the good quality spectra into MALDI Biotyper Explorer software (Bruker Daltonics) for processing, including smoothing, baseline subtraction, normalising, and peak selection

Perform cluster analysis using main spectrum profiles (MSP) dendrograms and composite correlation index (CCI) using MALDI-Biotyper v3.0 software to check the similarities between and among the spectra of the same and different categories
Database creation using MALDI-Biotyper [10,16]
Select the best quality spectra (based on an equitable algorithm on the MSP spectra' intensity, frequency, and peak position)
Select a minimum of ten spectra (from head and thorax) per mosquito sibling species with high spectral reproducibility to create the spectral database and increase discriminative power
Import the spectra in the MALDI-Biotyper software to create a reference database
Database query, blinds tests and validation using MALDI-Biotyper 3.0.
Select and load the MSP database of interest from the Taxonomy Tree View area
The MSPs are displayed on the MSP table, which can be used for classification or clustering
Load the rest of the spectra into the file view area of the MALDI-Biotyper 3.0 software
Select all the spectra and initiate the identification process by clicking the " start identification " button
The results are displayed on the " Spectrum Scores table ", which summarises the results of a classification of an unknown spectrum with 10 top hits displayed
Use the spot with the highest log score value (LSV). The MALDI Biotyper produces log score values (LSV) ranging from 0.000 to 3.000 (Table 1)
For ambiguous results use the weighted log score value [15], and modify accordingly
Calculate a weighted LSV by multiplying the actual LSV by the inverse of their position and then by summing up the weighted LSV (Figure 2)
Compute the weighted LSV for each sample spotted in quadruplicate and calculate the mean of the weighted LSV for the four spots
The sibling species with the highest mean weighted LSV is considered the probable sibling species identity [10]
creator: Jonathan Karisa
